learning: When implementing managed one-shot continuations in Zig, run the first step on stack and only box state after the first real suspension to preserve a zero-allocation fast path.
evidence: zig build bench reported alloc_calls=0 for the no-capture loop and zig build run-effect-state plus zig build run-generator both passed in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift
application: For future control-family features in shift, keep boxing behind the first suspension boundary and treat any no-capture allocation as a regression.

learning: When one-shot continuation wrappers can outlive their owner handle, prefer an explicit two-step Session lifecycle with close(mode) plus destroy() so teardown semantics stay safe without leaks or hidden global state.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while preserving alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, separate semantic shutdown from memory reclamation and keep destroy() gated on drained active continuations.

learning: When a two-step Session lifecycle is explicit, prefer one strict cleanup helper that fails loudly on unexpected close or destroy states because swallowed lifecycle errors can hide ownership bugs in continuation runtimes.
evidence: During the Session redesign in /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, strict cleanup plus the final green suite (zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, zig build bench) replaced earlier silent catch-based teardown.
application: For future shift lifecycle changes, route cleanup through one strict helper and treat SessionBusy or SessionOpen during expected teardown as a bug unless the test is explicitly asserting it.

learning: When splitting lifecycle ownership in a Zig continuation runtime, prefer a strict close-then-destroy Session API because it keeps semantic shutdown separate from memory reclamation and preserves zero-allocation steady-state proofs.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the Session rewrite passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench with alloc_calls=0 in the benchmark loop.
application: For future shift ownership changes, keep close semantics explicit, make destroy() validate lifecycle state, and re-check the no-capture benchmark after any owner-model change.

learning: When post-destroy continuation handles must fail cleanly in Zig, prefer a separate continuation control block plus explicit clone()/release() because plain struct copies are otherwise untrackable.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the continuation-control redesign passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-generator, and zig build bench while keeping alloc_calls=0 in the no-capture loop.
application: For future shift lifetime redesigns, move wrapper state out of the session owner and make extra handle references explicit instead of relying on invisible value copies.

learning: When hardening one-shot Zig continuation ownership, split the consuming owner handle from release-only aliases so active final release becomes impossible at the API surface and same-thread cleanup can be enforced.
evidence: zig build test passed after replacing clone/release with alias/release in src/control_spec.zig and src/raw.zig
zig build bench kept alloc_calls=0 after the owner/alias split
application: For future continuation lifecycle changes, make ownership states explicit in the public types before adding runtime guards.

learning: When tightening a documented unsafe Zig API, keep proven-used token names available behind a safer compatibility path so callers get a clear error instead of a silent surface break.
evidence: zig build test failed on the compatibility proof hook until Continuation.clone() and owner release() were restored with tightened semantics
zig build test and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed after reintroducing clone/release compatibility in src/control_spec.zig
application: For future safety hardening on exported surfaces, scan docs/examples first and prefer adapters or tightened compatibility methods before removing tokens outright.

learning: When one-shot continuation owners can survive close or destroy as tombstones, keep an explicit non-active owner drain path because removing it entirely leaks handles even if aliasing is otherwise strict.
evidence: zig build test initially leaked in the lifecycle matrix until closed and destroyed owners were explicitly drained via owner release()
zig build test, zig build run-effect-handlers, and zig build bench passed after keeping owner release() while removing clone()
application: For future lifecycle hardening in , treat active-owner duplication and non-active owner drainage as separate API decisions instead of deleting both together.

learning: When a continuation library starts accumulating one-off lifecycle tests, prefer a small reusable law harness because semantic drift becomes easier to catch and future ownership changes stop duplicating setup and drain logic.
evidence: zig build test passed after refactoring lifecycle coverage in src/control_spec.zig through LifecycleLawHarness
zig build run-effect-handlers and zig build bench both passed after promoting EffectSpec and interpreter composition in the public-facing wave
application: For future semantic waves in shift, add new lifecycle or effect laws by extending the shared harness and showcase instead of writing isolated scenario tests.

learning: When linking assembly context-switch stubs through Zig build modules, add the .S file only to the root module that owns the symbol; adding it again to importing example or bench modules produces duplicate symbol failures.
evidence: zig build failed with duplicate symbol definition: _shift_swap_context until build.zig stopped attaching the same aarch64_switch.S file to both the shift module and importing modules
application: Wire low-level assembly once per final artifact and keep example, bench, and test modules dependent on the already-linked library module.

learning: When a Zig direct-style shift/reset handler is meant to model true shift semantics, prefer returning the enclosing Answer and using continuation @"resume" only for the shift-expression Resume, because effect-style handler shapes otherwise trigger ContinuationNotConsumed and semantic drift.
evidence: examples/effect_state.zig initially failed with error.ContinuationNotConsumed until the example was rewritten so the handler returned the enclosing answer while @"resume" supplied the injected value
application: Shape new examples and helper APIs around answer-returning handlers whenever the runtime is claiming shift/reset semantics instead of explicit effect interpretation.

fingerprint.learning: When a nested stackful shift/reset runtime already has one driver loop per reset frame, prefer bubbling captured continuations through parent drivers before rewriting the entire runtime architecture, because that can unlock outer-prompt capture with a much smaller change set.
evidence: src/raw.zig now searches the parent fiber chain for the target prompt and bubbles captures upward; zig build test passes with a new outer prompt capture regression test
fingerprint.application: Before proposing another architectural restart, try routing captured continuations through the existing reset drivers and prove the semantics with a nested-prompt test.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful control runtime still creates a fresh top-level reset frame per call, prefer treating no-capture performance as a separate architecture problem after semantic fixes land, because the dominant cost remains the top-level context-switch path.
evidence: bench/no_capture_bench.zig output after the outer-prompt fix is iterations=50000 ns=235654958 checksum=1249975000, which is still far from the earlier managed-frame no-capture baseline
fingerprint.application: After semantic fixes, benchmark again and isolate top-level reset entry costs before assuming more control-semantic work will recover the no-capture fast path.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful shift/reset API needs typed user errors, prefer splitting the public surface into ControlError(ErrorSet) for shift-expression operations and ResetError(ErrorSet) for answer-producing paths, because nested reset calls otherwise force honest setup failures back into anyerror.
evidence: src/root.zig now exports ControlError and ResetError; zig build, zig build test, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, and the example runs all passed after the body and continuation-answer paths were widened to ResetError while shift itself stayed on ControlError
fingerprint.application: For future API tightening in this repo, preserve the control-vs-answer error split instead of collapsing the whole surface back to anyerror.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful runtime exposes public guard-release or deinit wrappers, prefer adding checked variants that return explicit lifecycle errors before keeping convenience wrappers, because silent cross-thread leave or deinit-during-reset is a misuse footgun.
evidence: src/raw.zig now exposes NoShiftGuard.leaveChecked() and Runtime.deinitChecked(); zig build test passes new misuse regressions for cross-thread guard release and deinit during an active reset
fingerprint.application: For future public lifecycle surfaces in this repo, ship an explicit checked API first and keep any void wrapper as a thin trap-only convenience around it.

fingerprint.learning: When a public stackful runtime exposes checked teardown, prefer giving the runtime an explicit destroyed state and rejecting reuse after successful deinit, because otherwise a second deinit or later reset can walk deinitialized storage.
evidence: src/raw.zig now tracks Runtime.state and zig build test passes a regression that expects error.RuntimeDestroyed on double deinitChecked() and reset() after teardown
fingerprint.application: For future lifecycle APIs in this repo, model terminal runtime states explicitly instead of relying on deallocated containers to fail implicitly.

fingerprint.learning: When prompt identity is only used for internal matching in a stackful shift/reset runtime, prefer a collision-free per-type token derived from a per-instantiation static marker instead of hashing @typeName(Tag), because hashing leaves a correctness risk that deterministic token equality can remove entirely.
evidence: src/raw.zig now uses PromptToken plus promptToken(comptime Tag) based on a per-type marker; zig build test passes new same-type, distinct-type, and cross-namespace token tests
fingerprint.application: For future internal identity keys in this repo, prefer exact token identity over probabilistic hashes whenever the key never needs serialization or stable external representation.

fingerprint.learning: When a stackful reset runtime keeps the continuation frame alive only until reset returns, prefer storing ResetFrame on the caller stack instead of heap-allocating it every call, because no-capture resets otherwise pay avoidable allocator cost on the hottest path.
evidence: src/raw.zig now initializes ResetFrame by value and zig build bench improved from iterations=50000 ns=219534167 checksum=1249975000 to iterations=50000 ns=5525792 checksum=1249975000
fingerprint.application: For future top-level reset optimizations in this repo, remove per-call frame heap allocation before attempting more invasive lazy-promotion or stack-switch changes.

fingerprint.learning: When a repo's default benchmark command is meant to guide performance work, prefer wiring that bench step to a dedicated ReleaseFast build of the target module, because benchmarking through the default debug optimize mode obscures real runtime progress.
evidence: build.zig now gives the bench step a ReleaseFast shift module; zig build bench reports iterations=50000 ns=2293417 checksum=1249975000 after the change versus 219534167 ns before the recent perf work and 5525792 ns after the reset-frame stack move alone
fingerprint.application: For future perf work in this repo, keep correctness gates on the default optimize mode but make benchmark entrypoints explicitly performance-oriented by default.

fingerprint.learning: When defining a lint-clean Zig ResumableSpec DSL, use 'tag' for the prompt marker but TitleCase type aliases like 'Answer', 'ErrorSet', 'ResumeValue', and 'Operation'; lowercase 'resume' collides with the language and the mixed casing satisfies zlinter.
evidence: zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 passed only after switching the new ResumableSpec examples/tests/benches to tag + TitleCase type aliases and avoiding lowercase resume.
fingerprint.application: For future Zig DSLs that expose a comptime spec struct, check zlinter naming classes early and design the declarative field names around them instead of retrofitting after the implementation compiles.

fingerprint.learning: When benchmarking a resumable Zig control engine that allocates continuation cells on suspension, use std.heap.smp_allocator for the benchmark runtime or the result is dominated by page_allocator syscall cost rather than the continuation protocol itself.
evidence: With page_allocator, 'zig build bench-first-suspend' measured about 228141459 ns/50000 while 'zig build bench-direct-first-suspend' was about 5643375 ns/50000; after switching the benchmark runtime allocator to std.heap.smp_allocator, resumable first-suspend dropped to about 936917 ns/50000 and beat the direct reference at about 5643375 ns/50000.
fingerprint.application: For future performance claims around resumable continuation allocation, pin the benchmark allocator explicitly and compare against a same-repo reference benchmark before judging the engine design.

fingerprint.learning: When a control library's product identity is direct-style delimited continuations, remove fast-path sidecar engines that require explicit machine reification, because keeping both surfaces blurs the contract and shifts docs and benchmarks away from the primary semantics.
evidence: After adding ResumableSpec, the repo narrative and benchmark defaults moved toward the resumable path; removing src/resumable_spec.zig and its resumable benches restored a single direct-style surface while zig build/test/lint/examples/bench all stayed green.
fingerprint.application: For future shift/reset roadmap choices, treat performance adapters as subordinate to the direct-style API or drop them entirely instead of letting them become a second product.

fingerprint.learning: When an escaping stackful shift/reset runtime moves reset frames from caller stack storage to heap-backed lifetime, add a runtime-local frame freelist immediately, because correctness-first heap frames can pass semantic tests while still blowing the no-capture benchmark budget.
evidence: The first escaping-core rewrite passed zig build/test/check/examples but regressed zig build bench from a 2373792 ns median baseline to 3862833 ns (+62.7%); after adding cached_frames reuse in src/raw.zig, zig build bench improved to a 2541167 ns median (+7.05%) while zig build bench-first-suspend stayed within budget at 6791667 ns (+28.91%).
fingerprint.application: For future lifetime-expanding runtime rewrites in this repo, measure the hot no-capture path immediately after the correctness pass and add reuse at the owning runtime boundary before chasing smaller micro-optimizations.

fingerprint.learning: When an escaping Zig shift/reset runtime already reuses frame and suspension storage, low-risk hot-path micro-optimizations can still improve the no-capture benchmark while failing to move the first-suspend median in a stable direction, so stop after one or two bounded tries and plan a structural suspension-path rewrite instead of stacking more cache-front tweaks.
evidence: A hot-slot cache attempt worsened no-capture and first-suspend medians and was rolled back. A lower-level init-path shave plus removing a duplicate internal runtime-owner check improved zig build bench from the current escaping baseline 2585416 ns median to 2379375 ns, but zig build bench-first-suspend still landed at 5564542 ns versus the 5355708 ns escaping baseline, so the tree was restored to the last good commit.
fingerprint.application: For the next performance pass in this repo, treat an unchanged or worse first-suspend median after two low-risk runtime tweaks as evidence that the remaining cost is structural, not cache-front incidental overhead.

fingerprint.learning: When a structural fallback rewrites an escaping shift/reset API to a stable session driver but still misses the first-suspend benchmark target, keep the fresh escaping baseline artifact and roll the code back immediately, because at that point the remaining path is no longer an implementation detail tweak but a larger product/semantics decision.
evidence: A fresh bench/baselines/escaping_step_v1.json captured a 5416917 ns bench-first-suspend median on c7d57c2. Phase A's internal suspension split stayed correct but missed the target, and Phase B's Session/Event redesign also stayed correct while landing around a 6584542 ns median for bench-first-suspend, so the code tree was restored and only the evidence artifact was kept.
fingerprint.application: For future shift/reset performance work in this repo, treat a failed stable-session redesign as a stop signal to reconsider the public model or workload assumptions before writing more code.

fingerprint.learning: When a one-shot shift/reset library pivots from runtime-oriented suspension mechanics to builder-facing lifecycle semantics, make cancellation a library-owned terminal branch with an explicit recovery error, because otherwise auto-cancel-on-drop and user-controlled discontinue stay semantically entangled and the public contract remains fuzzy.
evidence: The Outcome/Token rewrite kept explicit discontinue(err), added library-owned cancellation plus CancellationRecovered, and the full gate set stayed green after rewriting README, docs, tests, and examples to the new contract.
fingerprint.application: For future semantic redesigns in this repo, separate user-owned error injection from library-owned cancellation as early as the public API sketch, then enforce the distinction in both runtime errors and examples.

fingerprint.learning: When adding a larger real-world Zig example to a control-flow library, split the runnable entrypoint from an importable helper module and drive both stdout and tests through the same writer-based helper so README output, example behavior, and focused proofs cannot drift.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the new job_workflow example stayed lint-clean and passed zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build check, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity after examples/job_workflow/workflow.zig exposed reusable runScenario/runShowcase helpers for test/job_workflow_test.zig.
fingerprint.application: For future showcase examples in this repo, keep the executable thin, move deterministic behavior into an importable helper, and make tests capture the same rendered trace the README documents.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig showcase driver prints token payloads before resolving them, prefer errdefer token.deinit() around the writer call because otherwise a writer failure leaves the suspension unresolved and runtime teardown hits error.RuntimeBusy.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, a focused regression test in test/job_workflow_test.zig reproduced error.RuntimeBusy and leaked frame/suspension allocations after job_workflow.runScenario hit error.WriteFailed; after adding errdefer token.deinit() in examples/job_workflow/workflow.zig, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, and zig build run-job-workflow all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future shift examples and drivers, treat writer/logging failures in token-handling branches as ownership boundaries and drain unresolved tokens before propagating the I/O error.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig showcase example graduates from a transcript-only README snippet, prefer adding a short docs walkthrough that maps each printed branch to the exact control operation because that keeps implementation momentum while making the example usable as an orientation tool.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding docs/job_workflow.md plus a single README pointer passed zig build docs-sanity and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 without touching the runtime surface, and zig build run-job-workflow still matched the documented transcript.
fingerprint.application: For future examples in this repo, keep README output concise and move branch-by-branch explanation into docs once the example becomes large enough to hide the control-flow lesson.

fingerprint.learning: When a generic Zig helper around token resume, discontinue, and cancel paths passes tests but fails Debug example executables, prefer narrowing the rollout to the stable subset instead of forcing all example migrations through a toolchain bug.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the shared examples/support/driver.zig helper and its dedicated tests passed zig build test, but migrating generator, effect_state, and effect_handlers onto that helper triggered repeatable Debug executable LLVM failures during zig build run-generator and zig build run-effect-state. The stable rollout kept the helper plus tests and the job_workflow migration, while restoring the three small examples to their manual loops so zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future generic helper refactors in this repo, treat runnable example executables as a separate proof lane from tests and narrow scope when the helper is semantically sound but the current Zig toolchain is not.

fingerprint.learning: When a generic Zig helper over shift token decisions targets specs with and without user error sets, prefer specializing the decision type on an empty ErrorSet because otherwise Debug example builds can hit toolchain failures even when tests and richer examples pass.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the shared examples/support/driver.zig helper originally passed zig build test but generator and effect_state executable builds failed in Debug. After specializing Decision(Spec) and applyDecision for empty ErrorSet specs, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, and zig build docs-sanity all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future generic helper work in this repo, treat empty user error sets as a distinct compile-time case instead of carrying a dead discontinue branch through all specializations.

fingerprint.learning: When promoting a shared Zig workflow helper into a public API, keep it namespaced and additive, migrate only the workflow-style examples to it, and move teaching examples back to direct token loops so the helper does not silently become the canonical surface.
evidence: Added public helper exports in src/driver.zig and src/root.zig while deleting examples/support/driver.zig.

zig build test, zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, and zig build run-job-workflow all passed after the migration.
fingerprint.application: For future ergonomic API additions in continuation-style Zig libraries, treat the low-level contract as canonical and use selective example migration plus direct-output proof to show where the helper truly adds value.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig benchmark is supposed to prove a warmed steady-state path, prefer embedding warm-up plus multi-sample median collection in the benchmark binary and comparing candidate vs target on the same machine, because otherwise cold-start drift can create false regressions or hide real ones.
evidence: The original no-capture benchmark mixed runtime cache fill with the timed loop; repeated invocations drifted sharply before the harness changed to 20000 warmup iterations and 5 internal samples.
Same-machine A/B after the harness change passed the 5% gate: bench median 3222042ns vs 3164125ns (+1.83%), bench-first-suspend median 5481416ns vs 5726125ns (-4.27%).
fingerprint.application: For future perf-proof work in this repo, update the benchmark contract first, then require warmed internal medians plus same-machine A/B before treating a regression as real.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control runtime needs a research-heavy redesign, prefer adding a repo-grounded law set, machine correspondence, and decision dossier before changing the public API, because that turns broad theory into concrete surface decisions while keeping the current proof and benchmark lanes honest.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding docs/research_laws.md, docs/research_machine.md, docs/research_decision.md, and updating docs/research.md plus README.md kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build bench, and zig build bench-first-suspend all green.
fingerprint.application: For future semantics-heavy waves in this repo, land the law/machine/decision documentation slice first and use it to choose the next API cut instead of redesigning the surface directly from external PL literature.

fingerprint.learning: When a one-shot delimited-control API splits normal pending resolution from delayed escape, implement the split in the runtime first, then migrate the driver, examples, benchmarks, and docs in the same wave, because otherwise the build graph keeps compiling stale public-surface assumptions through install-dependent example and bench steps.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, changing Outcome.token/Token to Outcome.pending/Pending plus EscapedToken across src/raw.zig, src/root.zig, src/driver.zig, examples, benches, and docs kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build bench, and zig build bench-first-suspend green after bench/example compile failures exposed stale .token branches.
fingerprint.application: For future public-surface breaks in this repo, cut the semantic core first, then update every proof lane that depends on install/build artifacts before treating the new API as stable.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control API refactor keeps correctness green but leaves one warmed benchmark lane outside the prior guardrail, prefer writing an explicit perf-proof artifact before more tuning, because that preserves decision-quality evidence instead of silently normalizing the miss.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the pending-owner public API cut kept zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, and the example suite green, but a fresh bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v1.json recorded bench median 2953666ns (-8.33%) and bench-first-suspend median 5942666ns (+8.41%) against bench/baselines/direct_style_v2.json.
fingerprint.application: For future performance-sensitive API cuts in this repo, write a scoped proof artifact whenever one benchmark lane regresses beyond the standing threshold, then use that artifact as the starting point for the next optimization slice.

fingerprint.learning: When a microbenchmark lane is noisy enough that a single warmed invocation can flip a regression verdict, prefer checking in a repeated-invocation capture script and a p50-of-medians proof artifact, because that gives the repo a reproducible source of truth instead of one-off benchmark snapshots.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, adding bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh and bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v2.json showed the pending-owner API cut had bench p50 3062209ns (-4.96%) and bench-first-suspend p50 5459958ns (-0.39%) over 7 warmed invocations, replacing the earlier one-off artifact that had implied a first-suspend regression.
fingerprint.application: For future perf follow-ups in this repo, regenerate the repeated-run proof artifact before accepting or rejecting a regression on the warmed benchmark lanes.

fingerprint.learning: When a benchmark lane is noisy enough that one warmed invocation can invert the pass/fail verdict, prefer retiring the one-off proof artifact in favor of a repeated-run artifact, because future perf decisions should anchor on p50-of-medians rather than a single snapshot.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v1.json was retired after bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh and bench/baselines/pending_owner_api_perf_proof_v2.json showed the pending-owner first-suspend lane at -0.39% over 7 warmed invocations instead of the earlier one-off +8.41% artifact result.
fingerprint.application: For future noisy perf lanes in this repo, regenerate and point docs at repeated-run proof artifacts before accepting a regression narrative.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig API can only make some ownership misuses impossible, prefer hiding unsupported operations from the type surface and proving them with compile-fail fixtures wired into zig build test, because that raises the floor for normal callers while leaving the runtime backstops to cover the cases Zig cannot statically forbid.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, removing discontinue from Pending/EscapedToken when Spec.ErrorSet is empty and wiring test/compile_fail fixtures into build.zig kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build compile-fail, zig build test, zig build docs-sanity, the runnable examples, and both benchmark commands green.
fingerprint.application: For future API-hardening work in this repo, encode the strongest feasible restriction in the public type surface first, then add compile-fail fixtures for the now-unrepresentable misuse patterns before relying on runtime errors alone.

fingerprint.learning: When finishing a public terminology migration in shift, prefer renaming the public type and its alias-misuse error together and proving the old surface is gone with a compile-fail fixture plus a repo grep, because mixed old/new naming can survive a green positive-test suite.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, renaming EscapedToken->EscapedOwner and TokenAliased->OwnerAliased stayed green under 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build test', 'zig build docs-sanity', all four example runs, both bench commands, and 'rg -n "EscapedToken|TokenAliased|token lifecycle" README.md docs src test examples --glob '!test/compile_fail/escaped_token_removed.zig' --glob '!test/compile_fail/run.sh'' returned no matches.
fingerprint.application: For future public rename waves in this repo, pair the symbol rename with its related public error and doc language and add an explicit no-alias proof instead of relying on positive tests alone.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift public API has a resume transition whose payload type is void, prefer specializing the surface to payloadless names like proceed() and .proceed plus compile-fail guards, because forcing resumeWith({}) and .resume_value = {} keeps a meaningless value path alive in docs, examples, and callers.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, specializing Pending/EscapedOwner void resumes to proceed() and shift.driver.Decision(void_spec) to .proceed kept 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build test', 'zig build docs-sanity', the four runnable examples, and both benchmark commands green after adding compile-fail fixtures for Pending.resumeWith, EscapedOwner.resumeWith, and driver Decision.resume_value on void resume specs.
fingerprint.application: For future public API waves in this repo, derive the surface from whether a machine edge carries data; when it does not, remove the payload-shaped API and lock the specialization with compile-fail and example proof.

fingerprint.learning: When splitting a monolithic Zig runtime file without semantic drift, prefer a thin compatibility barrel over a delete-and-rename move, and prove the split with both the existing closure suite and an explicit public-surface diff audit, because green tests alone do not prove the facade stayed untouched.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, splitting src/raw.zig into src/raw_kernel.zig plus src/raw_surface.zig while keeping src/raw.zig as the barrel stayed green under 'zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0', 'zig build test', 'zig build compile-fail', 'zig build docs-sanity', the four runnable examples, both benchmark commands, and 'git diff --name-only -- src/root.zig src/driver.zig README.md docs examples test/compile_fail test/example_driver_test.zig test/size_check.zig' returned no output.
fingerprint.application: For future internal refactors in this repo, keep the old raw entrypoint stable first, move only the private-internal tests that truly need relocation, and require a no-drift file-surface audit before calling the split safe.

fingerprint.learning: When a warmed benchmark gate is symmetric around a fixed baseline, repeated proof reruns can fail because the candidate is materially faster; prefer confirming multi-run stability before refreshing the tracked proof artifact or declaring closure.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift, the five-idea wave closure suite passed, but three repeated runs of bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh produced no-capture delta_pct values of -4.88, -5.93, and -6.88 while bench_first_suspend stayed within threshold; st-008 remains in_progress with the blocker recorded in .step/st-plan.jsonl.
fingerprint.application: For future shift perf-gated turns, rerun the warmed proof multiple times and treat a symmetric >5% improvement as a policy question or baseline-refresh candidate before overwriting the tracked JSON artifact.

fingerprint.learning: When a delimited-control docs wave is meant to constrain future API stories, add one escaped-owner witness that resumes later and suspends again; it proves re-delimitation more directly than prose alone.
evidence: Added src/raw_surface.zig test 'escaped owner delayed resolution re-delimits resumed work' and then passed zig build test, zig build size-check, zig build compile-fail, zig build docs-sanity, and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 on 2026-03-10.
fingerprint.application: On future shift/reset semantic or documentation waves, anchor the narrative on one executable delayed-escape trace before refining the machine or public API docs.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig repo wants executable semantic claims, prefer a Zig-native witness registry plus transcript-locked runner over docs-only narratives, because it keeps examples, tests, and entrypoints aligned under one source of truth.
evidence: In /Users/tk/workspace/tk/shift on 2026-03-10, adding src/control_lab/registry.zig, src/control_lab/scenarios.zig, examples/control_studio/main.zig, test/control_studio_test.zig, docs/control_lab.md, and build.zig wiring kept zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0, zig build control-studio-check, zig build test, zig build run-generator, zig build run-effect-state, zig build run-effect-handlers, zig build run-job-workflow, zig build run-control-studio -- list, and bench/capture_pending_owner_perf_proof.sh green.

fingerprint.learning: When a major Zig API reset is blocked by legacy test declarations inside imported modules, prefer executable smoke checks as the first proof lane because they let the new surface land before every embedded test is rewritten.

fingerprint.learning: When narrowing a Zig continuation library from a broader control runtime to a smaller shipped surface, remove legacy branches from the public outcome and owner types first because that forces examples, benches, and smoke checks onto the real product contract immediately.

fingerprint.learning: When a continuation library claims full linearity through the runtime, require escaped-owner deinit to free owned continuation data directly because any hidden cancellation or stack re-entry means the runtime is still not first-order.

fingerprint.learning: When a narrowed Zig library keeps an old runtime tree that is no longer referenced by code, tests, or build wiring, remove the archived tree and its last doc pointer once lint and test still pass.

fingerprint.learning: When rewriting the top of a technical library README for contributor orientation, prefer replacing the old intro block with one named-surface purpose section plus one explicit non-goal sentence, because stacking both versions creates duplicate framing and makes old runtime assumptions linger.

fingerprint.learning: When a control-effect API claims a guarantee, treat any divergence between the public surface, the runtime enforcement path, and the proof harness as a correctness bug; align all three before optimizing ergonomics or performance, because otherwise the repo will silently overclaim what is actually enforced.

Performance work remained valid only after the semantic boundary was truthful; prompt-local fast-path optimization then improved the benchmark without changing the strengthened guarantee surface.
fingerprint.application: For future effect families or control abstractions, audit claim/runtime/tests separately first, then harden the weakest boundary before treating benchmark wins as meaningful.

evidence: Forged state-context and cross-instance reader misuse compile probes both failed under the current tree, while zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 and zig build test both passed

fingerprint.application: For future trust passes, state the strongest bounded claim that the proof surface supports, then list remaining drift explicitly instead of forcing a false all-or-nothing verdict.

fingerprint.learning: When a control-abstraction repo already has witness tests and a hand-maintained law document, prefer a small typed registry plus a generator-owned checked artifact because that removes the last doc-drift surface without duplicating transcript truth.
evidence: In shift, moving FORMAL_CORE.md behind src/formal_core_registry.zig and zig build formal-core/formal-core-write kept semantic-manifest anchors live while eliminating grep-only ownership checks.
fingerprint.application: Use this when docs are supposed to state only what witnesses and compile-fail fixtures already prove; keep transcripts where they are and generate only the law surface and provenance footer.

fingerprint.learning: When a one-shot effect runtime needs bracketed cleanup across abortive exits, prefer a type-erased cleanup stack driven from the aborting handlers because post-reset cleanup in abandoned intermediate fibers is not reliable.
evidence: In shift, resource cleanup only became truthful for outer exception and optional aborts after cleanup.zig let those handlers unwind active resource frames before completing the abort.
fingerprint.application: Use this when adding lifecycle effects on top of stackful one-shot control; prove cross-family abort cleanup before documenting the guarantee.

fingerprint.learning: When a repo already has one deep family-specific performance artifact, prefer adding a second matrix artifact for breadth instead of replacing the old lane because decomposition history and full-surface coverage serve different truth functions.
evidence: In shift, state already had a dedicated state_effect benchmark and artifact, while reader, optional, exception, and resource had no checked cost surface; adding bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig closed coverage without erasing the richer state history.
fingerprint.application: Use this when multiple shipped variants need measured coverage but one existing lane already carries deeper historical decomposition; keep that lane and add a matrix for breadth.

fingerprint.learning: When a benchmark matrix compares effect families against raw comparators, treat extreme outliers as comparator-design evidence first because tiny direct lanes can exaggerate fixed wrapper overhead beyond what the family would show under realistic work.
evidence: In shift, state stayed below raw, reader/optional/exception clustered near modest fixed overhead, but resource_normal jumped above 10x because its direct comparator was still far cheaper than the effect lane despite matching checksums.
fingerprint.application: Use this when expanding performance coverage across multiple control paths; stress-test comparator fairness and workload size before treating a large ratio as an architectural regression.

fingerprint.learning: When generalizing one-shot effect construction, add a hard boundary that forbids family modules from touching raw control primitives directly because otherwise a shared helper layer can still hide bespoke runners behind a generalized story.
evidence: In shift, the generalized claim only became falsifiable once the public family modules were routed through algebraic.zig and the new effect-construction-boundary check could fail on direct raw imports or calls.
fingerprint.application: Use this when a codebase already has several strong families and you need to prove the next family is routine; make the construction boundary executable before claiming the substrate is generalized.

fingerprint.learning: When a Zig benchmark file only fails declaration-length and doc-comment lint, prefer shortening internal identifiers and adding local adapter docs while leaving emitted lane names, lane_class values, and threshold constants semantically unchanged.
evidence: zig build lint -- --include bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig --max-warnings 0 -> No issues!
zig build bench-effect-matrix --summary none -> header still reports lanes=14 schema_version=2 and all 14 lane lines include lane_class
fingerprint.application: For future benchmark-lint cleanup in shift, keep edits file-scoped and validate both lint and the emitted benchmark header/lane contract before stopping.

fingerprint.learning: When a family benchmark matrix has both tiny-body fixed-tax lanes and suspicious outliers, split it into explicit micro, amortized, and investigation lanes because otherwise one artifact conflates stable cost, amortized cost, and unresolved comparator problems.
evidence: In shift, a v2 effect-family matrix with reader/optional/exception companion lanes and resource/writer investigation lanes made the high ratios interpretable without erasing the original micro-cost signal.
fingerprint.application: Use this when a benchmark matrix mixes ordinary wrapper overhead with clearly suspicious comparator or scaling cases; keep the lanes separate and label them in the checked docs and artifact.

fingerprint.learning: When a benchmark matrix has both fixed-tax lanes and still-suspicious outliers, split the artifact into explicit micro, amortized, and investigation classes because that lets the coverage surface stay truthful without pretending every high-threshold lane is a success case.
evidence: In shift, recutting the family matrix to v2 with writer and resource investigation lanes kept the artifact green while making it explicit which ratios are still diagnostic rather than settled performance claims.
fingerprint.application: Use this when performance coverage must expand before every outlier is optimized; keep the artifact honest by labeling lane intent directly in the checked output and generated docs.

fingerprint.learning: When prompt-local resume_then_transform operations already mutate active-frame state before resuming, prefer a true raw-internal local identity return over building a same-fiber capture/resume cycle, because in shift this collapsed the shared per-operation tax across state, reader, resource, and batched writer lanes without changing the compile-fail or exact-output proof surface.
evidence: After changing src/raw.zig shiftLocalIdentity to validate-and-return and routing src/effect/algebraic.zig acquireResource through it, zig build test and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 still passed.
zig build bench-effect-matrix reduced resource_normal_32 from about 43.42x to about 5.52x and writer_batch64 from about 17.10x to about 2.41x on the same machine.
The per-op extra cost dropped from about 51.5ns to 5.45ns for resource_normal_32 and from about 42.8ns to 3.56ns for writer_batch64.
fingerprint.application: For future transform-family perf passes, first look for prompt-local identity-afterResume paths that can return directly after exact-context validation before tuning allocator growth or family-local APIs.

fingerprint.learning: When strict prompt-local transform families in shift no longer use real control capture on their public operations, the remaining high writer/resource cost is often the family handle itself; replacing the transform-family handle and read/write/mutate path with direct active-frame execution can collapse the residual overhead far beyond substrate micro-tuning, but only if the benchmark harness also uses doNotOptimizeAway so LLVM cannot erase the new direct path.
evidence: After removing raw.reset from src/effect/family.zig handle, removing raw control from prompt-local transform ops in src/effect/algebraic.zig, and adding preserveValue/std.mem.doNotOptimizeAway barriers in both bench/state_effect_bench.zig and bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig, zig build test and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 still passed.

With the measurement barrier in place, zig build bench-effect-matrix moved resource_normal_32 to about 1.84x and writer_batch64 to about 1.12x, versus the earlier post-substrate-cut values around 5.52x and 2.41x.

zig build bench-state-effect now reports effect median about 23.0us versus raw median about 9.53ms, showing the transform-family path is effectively direct active-frame execution rather than reset-driven control.
fingerprint.application: For future transform-family perf work in shift, treat raw control substrate optimization and transform-family direct execution as two separate levers, and harden the benchmark against optimizer erasure before trusting extreme wins.

fingerprint.learning: When a repo carries multiple checked benchmark artifacts, the artifact scripts should compute repo cleanliness and allowed git_rev drift against the whole benchmark-baseline set rather than only their own output file, because otherwise a legitimate same-wave baseline refresh deadlocks on dirty-tree and git-rev checks even when the code under test is already clean.
evidence: After updating bench/state_effect_artifact.sh and bench/effect_family_matrix_artifact.sh to exclude both bench/baselines/state_effect_v1.json and bench/baselines/effect_family_matrix_v2.json from repo-state and git-diff comparisons, zig build bench-state-effect-check and zig build bench-effect-matrix-check both succeeded in the same dirty tree containing both refreshed baselines.

The same closure wave also added a first-item fast path in src/effect/writer.zig and hardened the writer benchmark to consume materialized slices, moving writer_micro below raw on repeated warmed runs.
fingerprint.application: For future multi-artifact benchmark repos, treat baseline files as one proof surface and validate cross-artifact refreshes in a single wave before shipping script changes.

fingerprint.learning: When a writer-style first-slot storage optimization targets a different effect family, keep it only if the representative amortized lane moves; in shift the same trick did not materially improve resource_normal_32, so the right move was to reject the patch and keep the previously proven transform-family and writer gains.
evidence: A prompt-local first-resource fast path in src/effect/algebraic.zig left the meaningful resource_normal_32 lane roughly unchanged-to-worse on repeated warmed matrix runs, even though the tiny resource_normal_4 lane nudged slightly.
After reverting that experiment, git status returned clean and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 plus zig build test still passed.
fingerprint.application: For future effect perf waves, treat writer-style small-storage tricks as family-specific until the representative benchmark lane proves they transfer.

fingerprint.learning: When an effect benchmark surface mixes direct-frame lanes, storage-backed lanes, and abortive-control lanes, add private decomposition benches before touching the checked artifact contract, because the decomposition can prove which candidate optimizations should be rejected even when the family names look similar.
evidence: In shift, writer/resource/abortive decomposition benches showed that optional/exception only needed acceptance evidence, resource inline cleanup storage did not improve the representative 32-item lane enough to keep, and writer inline16 improved small lanes but not the large representative lane enough to justify retention.
The accepted code change from this wave was the optional return-now fast check in src/effect/algebraic.zig; writer/resource storage experiments were measured and rejected.
fingerprint.application: For future shift perf waves, keep the checked matrix stable and use decomposition benches as the decision gate for family-specific experiments before refreshing baselines.

fingerprint.learning: When adding a public one-shot algebraic builder layer on top of shift/reset, prefer making `afterResume` depend only on handler state plus the resumed answer, because carrying the original perform payload across the resumed body either forces hidden storage or breaks the no-allocation/comptime-friendly path.
evidence: src/algebraic.zig: transform/choice handlers were tightened so `afterResume` receives `(State, Answer)` instead of `(State, Payload, Answer)`
zig build test -> ok after moving binding identity to prompt lifetime and dropping payload from afterResume
fingerprint.application: For future public effect builders on this runtime, reject APIs that require handler callbacks to retain per-perform payload across the resumed body unless that storage strategy is explicit and proven.

fingerprint.learning: When benchmarking a new public one-shot algebraic builder surface, prefer adding raw same-mode comparators to the existing checked effect matrix instead of inventing a separate benchmark harness, because that preserves the current proof contract and makes overhead visible relative to the exact control operator being lowered.
evidence: bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig now adds algebraic_transform_micro, algebraic_choice_return_now_micro, and algebraic_abort_micro against raw same-mode prompts
SHIFT_ALLOW_DIRTY_ARTIFACT=1 sh bench/effect_family_matrix_artifact.sh check -> ok after lane additions and threshold tuning
fingerprint.application: For future public control surfaces in shift, extend the checked matrix in-place with same-mode raw comparators before claiming acceptable overhead.

fingerprint.learning: When a public one-shot algebraic builder stores per-perform payload in threadlocal state, prefer storing a payload pointer and building an exact recursive binding chain instead of copying payload values into TLS or wrapping bindings in optionals, because that removes one hot-path copy and keeps the runtime representation closer to the comptime-declared op set.
evidence: src/algebraic.zig now uses `active_payload_ptr: ?*const Op.Payload` and `BindingChainType`/`BindingAtType` instead of copied payload TLS and optional-wrapped tuples
zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 -> ok; zig build test -> ok; SHIFT_ALLOW_DIRTY_ARTIFACT=1 sh bench/effect_family_matrix_artifact.sh check -> ok
fingerprint.application: For future shift algebraic/runtime work, treat payload TLS copies and optionalized exact-arity binding storage as zero-cost regressions unless a benchmark proves they are required.

fingerprint.learning: When adding a proof-only parity backend to shift, prefer a separate parity machine plus import-friendly run(writer) example entrypoints over touching the public runtime path; it preserves the comptime/zero-cost product surface while still giving a real backend-parity gate.
evidence: zig build backend-parity passed after adding src/parity_machine.zig, test/backend_parity_manifest.zig, and test/backend_parity_test.zig.
zig build test stayed green after witnesses switched to package import and the docs/formal-core were updated for backend-parity.
fingerprint.application: For future proof backends in semantics-first Zig repos, land the parity corpus and proof-only runner first, and only consider public/runtime seams after the proof backend has earned its way in.

fingerprint.learning: When upgrading a transcript-first parity backend toward a more real defunctionalized kernel in shift, prefer migrating one owned slice to typed state checkpoints before widening coverage because transcript equality alone can hide string replay and fail to prove stateful semantics.
evidence: zig build backend-parity passed after the six witness cases and nested_workflow moved onto src/parity_kernel.zig while untouched cases stayed on src/parity_machine.zig.
zig build test, zig build readme-contract, and zig build formal-core stayed green after coexistence routing and state-ledger fixtures landed.
fingerprint.application: For future parity-kernel upgrades in shift, add a state-trace fixture the same turn a case moves to the typed kernel and keep manifest engine ownership explicit until the legacy path is retired.

fingerprint.learning: When migrating a transcript-first parity case onto a typed proof kernel in shift, prefer adding typed state checkpoints in the same change because exact transcript parity alone can hide that the new engine is still replaying strings instead of executing stateful semantics.
evidence: zig build backend-parity passed after src/parity_kernel.zig took ownership of the six witness cases and nested_workflow while untouched cases stayed on src/parity_machine.zig.
zig build test, zig build readme-contract, and zig build formal-core stayed green after the coexistence routing and state-ledger fixtures landed.
fingerprint.application: For future kernel migrations in shift, treat transcript parity as necessary but insufficient: a case is not migrated until it also has an explicit typed state-trace fixture.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift benchmark lane is either a near-empty fixed-tax micro path or an inline-capacity boundary case, prefer decomposition or investigation retiering before blaming runtime regressions, because those shapes are measurement-sensitive and can look noisy without any core hot-path change.

bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig measures algebraic_abort_micro as an almost-empty direct-return micro lane, while bench/algebraic_builder_decompose_bench.zig only decomposes transform shell/perform cost, not abort.

fingerprint.learning: When an effect-matrix lane is flaky on unchanged clean-tree code in shift, add a repeated clean-tree stability harness first because it separates true regressions from measurement noise and makes any later lane-policy change evidence-backed instead of speculative.

That same run left bench-state-effect-check green and showed the remaining matrix issue was lane policy, not broad public-path regression.
fingerprint.application: For future matrix regressions in shift, require bench-effect-matrix-stability output before changing thresholds or lane classes, then refresh checked artifacts only after stability is demonstrated.

fingerprint.learning: When a direct-style continuation repo keeps shift/reset as product truth, split positive unchanged-body bridge parity from negative boundary tests and rename scorecards away from candidate/productization framing because otherwise the lowered path can look like a second public model instead of hidden support infrastructure.

fingerprint.learning: When rerunning checked benchmark artifacts after a semantics-facing refactor in shift, use the clean-tree stability harness first and refresh both checked JSONs in one pass because otherwise a new git_rev stamp can hide whether the matrix itself is still stable.

zig build one-shot-survey, zig build compile-fail, zig build runtime-contract-suite, zig build readme-contract, zig build canonical-raw-dependency-matrix-check, zig build shipped-surface-frontier-matrix-check, and zig build test all passed after the split.

fingerprint.learning: When designing a lexical front door for shift, avoid storing bound effect operations as runtime fields whose function-pointer types still depend on comptime Cap/ErrorSet generics, because Zig treats those handle structs as comptime-only and the bundle cannot exist as mutable runtime state.

fingerprint.application: For the next lexical-front-door attempt, either erase operation dispatch to non-generic runtime call signatures or build the front door around a different binding model instead of runtime-stored generic function pointers.

fingerprint.learning: When migrating shift away from a public PromptMode story, infer generated effect family mode from declared ops and keep explicit .mode accepted only as a checked transitional override, because that reduces public PromptMode pressure without silently reinterpreting existing definitions.

fingerprint.learning: When building a lexical front door for shift, prefer slot-tagged handle values that store only the typed context pointer and dispatch through the existing lowered-machine/effect seams, because that keeps the handle runtime-storable and avoids the comptime-only failure caused by runtime-stored generic operation closures.

fingerprint.learning: When extending shift lexical handles to output-producing built-ins, keep shift.with(...) value-only and add a sibling withCollected(...) that accumulates descriptor outputs into a mirrored plain-data bundle during recursive unwind.

fingerprint.learning: When tightening shift's ordinary tool contract around emitted JSON and canonicalized accepted paths, prefer repo-local shell assertions over new external binaries and normalize accepted GeneratedProgram.source_path to the canonical repo-owned case path, because tool-path dependencies and machine-specific absolute paths both regress the public artifact surface.

fingerprint.application: On future ordinary tool contract edits, avoid introducing non-repo executables into the default test path and canonicalize accepted artifact metadata once the input has already been normalized to a supported case.

fingerprint.learning: When hardening shift's ordinary lowering surface for out-of-tree builds and external-cwd use, prefer rooting build-time canonical-source reads in the package root and resolving repo-relative inspected source paths against that same package root, because using the caller cwd in either place makes dependency builds or documented canonical paths fail outside the repo root.
evidence: zig build ordinary-tool-contract passed after anchoring build.zig canonicalSourceHash to b.pathFromRoot(...) and resolving relative inspectSource inputs through build_options.package_root before reading files.
zig build test --summary all passed with 135/135 steps and 91/91 tests after the package-root resolution change in src/ordinary_zig_lowering.zig.
fingerprint.application: On future ordinary lowerer path work, keep build-time asset reads and repo-relative runtime inputs explicitly anchored to the package root, while continuing to validate canonical case identity from the resolved source path itself.

fingerprint.learning: When validating shift's shared-source witness cases, prefer requiring the selected witness function signature inside entry_required_snippets and proving wrong-entry mismatches explicitly, because shared files can otherwise satisfy file-level snippets while lowering the wrong top-level function as canonical.
evidence: zig build test --summary all passed with 135/135 steps and 95/95 tests after adding witness function signatures to shared-source witness entry_required_snippets in src/ordinary_zig_lowering.zig and boundary regressions for the two reported wrong-entry witness pairs.
zig build ordinary-tool-contract stayed green after the witness entry tightening.
fingerprint.application: On future shared-source ordinary lowerer cases, include the selected function signature in the entry-bound checks and add a boundary test for each wrong-entry pair the reviewer can name.

fingerprint.learning: When exposing shift's ordinary lowerer as a public API/tool, prefer caller-cwd semantics for relative source_path values and treat expected_status as a real acceptance contract with a truthful default, because package-root-relative reads and ignored status knobs make the interface surprising even when the in-repo tests are green.
evidence: zig build ordinary-tool-contract passed after adding a tool-contract case that runs from examples/ with --source define_basic.zig and expects canonical JSON output.
zig build test --summary all passed with 135/135 steps and 96/96 tests after changing Spec.expected_status to default canonical and rejecting mismatches in inspectSourceText.
fingerprint.application: On future public ordinary-surface changes, keep relative path behavior aligned with normal CLI cwd semantics and either enforce any exported expectation fields or remove them before shipping.

fingerprint.learning: When finishing a squash-merge PR in shift with local .learnings.jsonl edits, prefer stashing only .learnings.jsonl before branch cleanup, because gh pr merge can complete the GitHub merge but fail the local checkout/delete step if that tracked file is dirty.
evidence: gh pr merge 6 --squash --delete-branch merged PR #6 at 2026-03-17T19:15:39Z but aborted local cleanup with 'Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by checkout: .learnings.jsonl'.

Stashing only .learnings.jsonl, switching to main, fast-forwarding to merge commit 96d4bfc, deleting the remote branch, force-deleting the local branch, and popping the stash restored the local learning edits on main.
fingerprint.application: On future runs in this repo, isolate .learnings.jsonl in a targeted stash before local branch cleanup when the branch is otherwise ready to merge.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift generated-family lexical surface needs inferred handler errors, prefer unioning op-method and after-hook return error sets by method signature, because preview-time AnswerType guesses can break valid lexical handle instantiations even when the direct handle path still compiles.

fingerprint.learning: When shift inferred-error fixes touch explicit performProgram helpers, derive prompt error sets from the continuation return type and prove them on shift.algebraic.Program(...).run, shift.effect.Define(...).handle(...), and proof.exampleHarness(...), because otherwise top-level explicit program(...) bodies silently drop apply(...) errors even when the main test bundle looks green.

fingerprint.learning: When shift ordinary lowering emits ErrorWitnessV1, derive setup and semantic error names from the canonical case and surface instead of stamping empty defaults, and mirror rejection diagnostics into error_witness.diagnostics, because otherwise the shipped ordinary JSON/Zig artifacts produce false negatives even while the main test bundle stays green.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift inferred-error change adds metadata like shift.With(...).SemanticErrorSet or widens one built-in direct handle, prefer deriving the metadata from the authored error union before ResetError(...) and proving every sibling public widening path, because otherwise runtime/setup failures leak into introspection metadata and one built-in surface can silently lag behind the others.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift metadata surface tries to subtract infrastructure errors by name, prefer preserving ambiguous all-infrastructure-name body unions and prove both the collision case and the non-collision filtering case, because otherwise SemanticErrorSet can introduce false negatives for user errors that share public runtime/setup names.

fingerprint.application: For future shift introspection metadata, prove one collision case and one propagated-runtime case before finalizing any name-based semantic/execution split.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift review says hidden protocol errors are 'gone' but public ResetError(...) still carries them, prefer aligning the declared public contract and witness artifacts back to the actual public type surface unless you also complete the deeper internal/public error split, because partial metadata-only removals make RuntimeError and ErrorWitnessV1 unreliable.
evidence: The review found shift.RuntimeError and ErrorWitnessV1.public_runtime_errors had been narrowed while lowered_machine.ControlError/ResetError still exposed FrontendSuspend and ProgramContractViolation through public APIs; after restoring those names in src/lowered_machine.zig, src/error_witness.zig, src/runtime_error_surface_registry.zig, docs/runtime_error_surface_matrix.json, and keeping the mixed-collision SemanticErrorSet proof in src/with_api.zig, sh test/public_error_api_ban/run.sh, sh test/ordinary_error_witness/run.sh, and zig build test all passed.
fingerprint.application: For future shift public-surface migrations, either remove a hidden error from every public type alias and call boundary in one wave or keep the metadata/registry contract aligned to the still-public type.

fingerprint.learning: When shift.With(...).SemanticErrorSet cannot distinguish semantic body errors from effect-propagated infrastructure names, prefer an explicit body-level SemanticErrorSet source of truth and fail closed for ambiguous effectful bodies, because type-only heuristics cannot preserve collided names and maintain a strict semantic/execution split at the same time.
evidence: The latest review found lexical performProgram previews dropped continuation errors in src/with_api.zig and mixed collided body errors became indistinguishable once effect operations injected the full lowered infrastructure set; after adding explicit-program continuation preview helpers, a body-level SemanticErrorSet override, and proof hooks in test/lexical_with_test.zig, zig build test passed.
fingerprint.application: For future shift metadata surfaces, add an explicit semantic-error declaration when the final inferred error union mixes authored and propagated infrastructure names instead of relying on name-subtraction heuristics.

fingerprint.learning: When shift.With(...).SemanticErrorSet needs to stay usable for effect-only bodies and preserve collided semantic names, prefer a body-declared SemanticErrorSet override plus a non-failing default for pure infrastructure-only bodies, because generic name-based inference cannot distinguish collided authored names from effect-propagated infrastructure names in every case.
evidence: The latest review found effect-only bodies like `try eff.state.get(); return 0;` were blocked by a compile error and rejected emitted ordinary Zig still needed owned witness diagnostics; after updating src/with_api.zig, tools/shift_ordinary_lower.zig, test/lexical_with_test.zig, and test/ordinary_tool_contract/run.sh, sh test/ordinary_tool_contract/run.sh and zig build test both passed.
fingerprint.application: For future shift metadata surfaces, keep an explicit semantic source-of-truth path for ambiguous bodies and let effect-only bodies fall back to an instantiable default instead of failing the companion type.

fingerprint.learning: When shift ordinary lowering emits ErrorWitnessV1, prefer deriving accepted and rejected witness metadata from the actual generated-artifact execution boundary instead of reusing runtime/frontend or canonical-case metadata, because otherwise accepted ordinary artifacts can advertise impossible runtime failures and rejected artifacts can falsely attribute semantic errors to unvalidated sources.

fingerprint.application: For future shift lint/toolchain regressions, treat zlinter and zls as a versioned proof dependency: keep the vendored package pin aligned with the working zls API and keep lint excludes scoped to repo-owned sources rather than vendored/cache trees.

fingerprint.learning: When renaming nested public Zig API members in shift, keep compatibility aliases for old names if the old surface was already public, because internal call-site updates alone do not protect downstream consumers from compile breaks.
evidence: 2026-03-26 review: src/program_api.zig renames Decl.family(...).Generated -> generated and Program.InternalManifest -> internal_manifest while the old names existed on the public API before this patch.
fingerprint.application: For future public-surface cleanup waves, add alias members such as Generated = generated and InternalManifest = internal_manifest before removing older spellings.

fingerprint.learning: When shift removes a vendored zlinter tree, keep the upstream dependency pin and exclude .zig-cache plus .zig-global-cache from the lint path set so fetched and generated code do not explode the lint gate.
evidence: build.zig.zon restored zlinter URL/hash, vendor/zlinter removed, and zig build lint -- --max-warnings 0 returned 'No issues!' on 2026-03-26.
fingerprint.application: On future zlinter cleanup or dependency de-vendoring in shift, prefer cache-path exclusions over vendoring third-party source into the branch diff.

fingerprint.learning: When renaming public root or family metadata names in shift, patch compatibility aliases and proof gates together.
evidence: zig build size-check failed on missing shift.Op and missing Generated/InternalManifest aliases before the compatibility patch.
zig build example-proof required adding front_door_workflow.zig to test/example_proof/run.sh so the new public example was actually covered.
fingerprint.application: Add compatibility aliases, update docs and proof-gate tools, update example-proof registries, then rerun size-check, source-lowering-gauntlet, public-error-api-ban, and example-proof.

fingerprint.learning: When shift must preserve lint-hostile compatibility aliases, isolate the alias names in tiny excluded shim files and keep the core implementation files lint-clean.
evidence: zig build lint failed on shift.Op-style lowercase aliases and Generated/InternalManifest namespace aliases until the compatibility names were moved out of src/op_api.zig and the core files were relinted.
fingerprint.application: For future compatibility shims in shift, prefer a small excluded shim module plus clean core wrappers, then rerun zig build lint, zig build, and zig build test.

fingerprint.learning: When shift authoring admission compares Zig sources via normalized token streams, prefer skipping ordinary slash-slash line-comment tokens in addition to doc comments because otherwise comment-only edits are rejected as structural drift.
evidence: src/internal/authoring_lowerer.zig:342-345 only skips .doc_comment and .container_doc_comment during token normalization
test/source_lowering_boundary_test.zig:154-185 asserts that comment-only edits to canonical fixtures are accepted
fingerprint.application: For future authoring_lowerer or bridge-admission refactors, preserve comment-insensitive matching in the shared tokenizer path to avoid regressing comment-only edits from accepted to rejected.

fingerprint.learning: When adding fail-closed bridge or source admission checks, pin canonical hashes to a checked-in baseline instead of recomputing them from the current checkout at build time, or canonical_source_drift will stop catching local edits.
evidence: build.zig now feeds authoring_lowerer_options.hash_* from canonicalSourceHash(...) over working-tree files.
src/internal/authoring_lowerer.zig compares the on-disk canonical file against those embedded hash_* values before reporting canonical_source_drift.
fingerprint.application: Store the admitted hashes in a committed artifact or generated source that is reviewed independently of the working tree being validated.

fingerprint.learning: When shift bridge admission shares the authoring lowerer across witness case ids and direct_style_bridge fixtures, prefer validating witness cases against src/witness_sources.zig and fixtures against wrapper-carried source/source_path because wrapper-only or case-id-only replay lets semantic drift pass the fail-closed boundary.
evidence: zig build direct-style-boundary -> ok after splitting source_module vs fixture_module and adding fixture hash coverage
zig build direct-style-bridge-parity -> ok after wrapper fixtures exported source/source_path
src/program_bridge.zig + src/direct_style_bridge_manifest.zig + test/direct_style_bridge_boundary_test.zig
fingerprint.application: Keep direct_style_bridge_manifest split between canonical authored source and fixture wrapper source, and require bridge fixtures to carry source plus source_path for fail-closed admission.

fingerprint.learning: When shift shares witness admission through authoring_lowerer.compare_scope=.entry, enumerate the actual shared witness entry functions and exclude only sibling witness bodies; keep shared helpers in scope, and free accepted LoweredAuthoring buffers before synthesizing an unsupported_shape rejection after the shape gate.
evidence: src/internal/authoring_lowerer.zig explicit shared witness entry-name filter
src/source_lowering.zig inspectSourceText/inspectFileBackedSourceText now deinit accepted lowered buffers before generatedRejectedProgram
zig test test/source_lowering_boundary_test.zig-equivalent module command passed 26/26 including whitespace-drift regression cases; zig build direct-style-boundary passed
fingerprint.application: For shared-source admission seams, make the include/exclude boundary explicit and add a whitespace-only regression that preserves AST admission but exercises any downstream text-shape rejection path under std.testing.allocator.

fingerprint.learning: When shift formatting is the only failing gate, run zig fmt --check over the tracked Zig files to get the exact drift set, format only the reported files, then rerun zig build and zig build test to confirm no behavioral regressions.
evidence: zig fmt --check reported four drifted files: test/lexical_with_test.zig, bench/effect_family_matrix_bench.zig, tools/check_public_api_ban.zig, tools/check_public_root_contract_snapshot.zig.
After zig fmt on those files, zig build, zig fmt --check, and zig build test all passed.
fingerprint.application: Use the repo-wide check to localize formatting-only failures quickly, keep the edit set minimal, and still close with the full build plus test proof bundle.

fingerprint.learning: When shift routes bridge lowering through program_bridge.canonicalBridgeCase, keep each bridge case's feature_flags aligned with its corresponding source-lowering case instead of hardcoding an empty slice, or lowering_equivalence_report.json will silently under-report bridge coverage.
evidence: src/program_bridge.zig:5-19 sets canonicalBridgeCase.feature_flags = &.{} for every bridge row
tools/render_lowering_equivalence_report.zig:271-296 serializes lowered.feature_flags for bridge rows
docs/lowering_equivalence_report.json:43-60 now shows [] for every bridge row feature_flags
fingerprint.application: When adding shared bridge/admission tooling in shift, preserve per-case bridge metadata in program_bridge so checked proof artifacts keep detecting coverage drift.

fingerprint.learning: When shift compares file-backed or inline authoring source paths against canonical cases, keep cwd-independent path resolution but canonicalize the resolved filesystem paths with realpath before equality; std.fs.path.resolve alone preserves symlink aliases and rejects valid canonical sources from symlinked checkouts.
evidence: src/internal/authoring_lowerer.zig now resolves caller paths and then canonicalizes both actual and expected paths with std.fs.realpathAlloc before sourcePathMatchesExpected equality.
test/source_lowering_boundary_test.zig now proves acceptance for absolute symlink aliases, inline alias paths, and repo-relative paths reached from a symlinked checkout.
fingerprint.application: For future authoring/source-lowering path work in shift, treat resolve-vs-realpath as two separate responsibilities: resolve for repo-root/cwd independence, realpath for filesystem identity checks.

fingerprint.learning: When shift validates canonical authoring source paths, prefer matching a canonical repo root plus normalized repo-relative suffix over full-file realpath equality because full-file realpath admits non-canonical file aliases while plain string equality rejects symlinked checkout roots.
evidence: src/internal/authoring_lowerer.zig now normalizes expected repo-relative paths to host separators, accepts only canonical repo paths or alias roots whose realpath is the repo root, and explicitly rejects repo-internal alias roots.

test/source_lowering_boundary_test.zig keeps external file-alias rejection, adds symlinked-checkout acceptance for repo-relative paths, and adds negative regressions for missing path boundaries and repo-internal checkout aliases.
fingerprint.application: For future shift path-admission work, canonicalize the repo root separately and require alias matches only at the checkout-root boundary.

fingerprint.learning: When a shift redesign touches the semantic core plus API, runtime, and lowering surfaces, prefer making the resolved semantic center the only initial durable blocker, because that unlocks a safe first parallel wave for API, runtime-adapter, and lowering work and avoids rework from shifting core contracts.
evidence: .step/st-plan.jsonl now models st-301 as the sole blocker and st-302/st-303/st-304 as the next dependency-independent wave.
fingerprint.application: For future shift rewrites, build the graph so the semantic center lands first and only then open the first parallel wave.
